name: calibur

metadata.name: calibur
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: Apache Software License
metadata.summary: Toolbox for converting rotations, camera calibration matrices, transforms and spaces.

documentation:
# Calibur🗡️

[![Read the Docs](https://img.shields.io/readthedocs/calibur?style=flat-square)](https://calibur.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)

Calibur, a sword towards the many inconsistent and ambiguous conventions
(viewport origins, depth range, camera coordinate systems, etc.) in computer graphics and vision.

It is a library that translates between these conventions for you.
It also hosts as a library of documentation of different conventions and space definitions in CG and CV
that would otherwise be hard to find.

## Installation

```bash
pip install calibur
```

## Documentation

The documentation is hosted [here](https://calibur.readthedocs.io/en/latest/).

functions.name: calibur.conventions.get_ruf_basis
documentation:
Get the *right/up/forward* basis matrix in `convention`.
The matrix is laid out as `[r|u|f]`.

functions.name: calibur.conventions.convert_pose
documentation:
Converts a pose between conventions.
Think of a pose as:

A small object has a fixed relative position to the camera.
The camera pose transforms the relative position into a world position.

`A @ p_c = p_w`

The conversion works as:

We have a camera with pose A in system A (say OpenGL).
Now we instead want a camera in system B (say OpenCV) to see the same scene.
What should be its pose?
We want to transform a point with same *right, up and forward* relative to the camera
into the same world point.
The answer would be `convert_pose(A, CC.GL, CC.CV)`.

```
param src_pose

(..., 4, 4) pose to convert.

param src_convention

Source convention of src_pose.
 or .

param dst_convention

Destination convention to convert into.
 or .

returns

(..., 4, 4) converted pose.
```

classes.name: calibur.conventions.CameraConventions
documentation:
Conventions of camera coordinate systems in *right, up, forward* order.
Also available as {py:data}`CC` for faster typing.

classes.name: calibur.conventions.WorldConventions

class_variables.name: calibur.conventions.WorldConventions.GL
documentation: TODO: double check

Conventions of world coordinate systems in *right, up, forward* order.

Currently experimental, as the definition of world forward is tricky.
The up axis is in general accurate and not ambiguous.

The current conventions for listing is:

* *Up* is for gravity up.
* *Forward* is: when you look into forward, you see the front of objects.
  This means that humanoid or animals walk in the backward direction.
* *Right* is: when you look into forward, the right of you.

As a result, when dealing with humanoids in game engines like unity,
*right/forward* can be the inverse direction to your expectations.
Unfortunately, this mirror effect exists in real-world and cannot be further unified.

classes.name: calibur.render_pipelines.SimpleRayTraceRP

methods.name: calibur.render_pipelines.SimpleRayTraceRP.set_geometry
documentation:
Set geometry triangles. Assumes CCW winding.

```
param tris

(N, 3, 3) triangles, array of 3 vertices.

returns

self after preprocessing the geometry.
```

methods.name: calibur.render_pipelines.SimpleRayTraceRP.render
documentation:
Renders the geometry using an environment.

```
param env

The  to shade the world.

param cam_pose_cv

(4, 4) camera pose in  conventions.

param fx

Focal length on the  (horizontal) axis.

param fy

Focal length on the  (vertical) axis.

param cx

Principal point  (horizontal) axis. Origin is top-left of frame.

param cy

Principal point  (vertical) axis. Origin is top-left of frame.

param sx

Sensor size  (horizontal), or width.

param sy

Sensor size  (vertical), or height.

param pixel_per_unit

If the previous focal lengths and sizes are in physical units like mm,
a value can be specified for pixel density per unit.
Defaults to 1.0.

returns

(H, W, 3) RGB image ranging in [0, 1],
where [H W] = round([sy sx] * pixel_per_unit).
```
A minimal CPU ray tracing render pipeline for debugging cameras.

functions.name: calibur.rays.get_dx_viewport_rays
documentation:
Shoot rays from `z_start` towards z +inf in viewport space.

Viewport *xy* lies in `(0-w, 0-h)`,
where `0/w/h` represent borders (instead of center of border pixels) of viewport.

The origin is top-left (vulkan/directx convention).

```
returns

a tuple (rays_o, rays_d), both of shape (h * w, 3).
```

functions.name: calibur.rays.get_view_ray_directions_cv
documentation:
---
param h: '`(int)` height of camera.'
param intrinsics: '`[fx, fy, cx, cy]` CV intrinsics of camera.'
param norm: whether the ray directions should be normalized.
param w: '`(int)` width of camera.'
returns: '`(h, w, 3)` directions of the rays in OpenCV camera coordinate'
---

functions.name: calibur.rays.transform_ray_directions_cv
documentation:
---
meta private: true
param c2w: |-
  `(3+, 4)` transformation matrix from camera coordinate to world coordinate,
  or the pose of the camera. Camera is in OpenCV convention.
param directions: '`(N, 3)` precomputed ray directions in camera coordinate.'
param norm: whether to normalize the resulting directions.
returns: a tuple `(rays_o, rays_d)`, both of shape `(N, 3)`.
---

functions.name: calibur.rays.get_cam_rays_cv
documentation:
Get camera rays (origin, dir) from extrinsics and intrinsics in OpenCV convention.

```
returns

a tuple (rays_o, rays_d).

rays_o -- (h * w, 3), the origin of the rays in world coordinates.

rays_d -- (h * w, 3), the normalized direction of the rays in world coordinates.
```

functions.name: calibur.viewport.gl_ndc_to_dx_ndc
documentation:
DX NDC differs to GL in that depth lies in `[0, 1]` instead of `[-1, 1]`.

This is a simple transform compressing the *Z* dimension of NDC.

functions.name: calibur.viewport.gl_ndc_to_gl_viewport
documentation:
GL NDC to GL viewport coordinates.

> Notice that GL viewport origin is the *bottom-left* corner.

If `near` and `far` are not None, the resulting *Z* is linear depth.
Otherwise *Z* is kept unchanged.

functions.name: calibur.viewport.gl_viewport_to_dx_viewport
documentation:
GL viewport to DX/Vulkan viewport coordinates.

    They differ in that DX viewport origin is *top-left* compared to *bottom-left* in GL.

functions.name: calibur.viewport.gl_ndc_to_dx_viewport
documentation:
GL NDC to DX viewport coordinates.

If `near` and `far` are not None, the resulting *Z* is linear depth.
Otherwise *Z* is kept unchanged.

functions.name: calibur.resources.get_blender_cube
documentation:
---
returns: triangles `(N, 3, 3)`.
---

functions.name: calibur.resources.get_spot
documentation:
The spot mesh from Keenan's 3D Model Repository
"Robust fairing via conformal curvature flow"
The mesh is in Unity coordinates (Z forward, Y up).
The animal looks into -Z.

```
returns

triangles (N, 3, 3).
```

functions.name: calibur.resources.get_monkey
documentation:
The blender monkey head mesh.
The mesh is in GL coordinates (-Z forward, Y up).
The animal looks into Z.

```
returns

triangles (N, 3, 3).
```

methods.name: calibur.raytracing.BVH.__init__
documentation:
Construct BVH from triangles.

```
param triangles

(N, 3, 3) array of 3 vertices.

param centroids

See note below.

param indices

See note below.
```

%

> Centroids and indices should only be passed if it is not the root node,
> which should not be used other than by the BVH itself in principle.

methods.name: calibur.raytracing.BVH.raycast
documentation:
Cast a batch of rays to the triangles in the BVH.

```
param rays_o

(N, 3) ray origins.

param rays_d

(N, 3) ray directions (normalized).

returns

a tuple (hit_i, hit_d, hit_u, hit_v).

hit_i -- (N) int32, index into given triangles, -1 if missing.

hit_d -- (N) float32, ray travel distances.

hit_u -- (N) float32, triangle barycentric u.

hit_v -- (N) float32, triangle barycentric v.

For barycentric coordinates, P = w v1 + u v2 + v v3.
```

classes.name: calibur.shading.Environment

methods.name: calibur.shading.Environment.shade
documentation:
---
param normals: '`(N, 3)` world-space normals.'
returns: '`(N, 3)` RGB values in `[0, 1]` range.'
---

Abstract base class for an shading environment.

Available implementations:
{py:class}`NormalCaptureEnvironment`,
{py:class}`SHEnvironment`,
{py:class}`SampleEnvironments`.

classes.name: calibur.shading.NormalCaptureEnvironment
documentation: A special 'environment' to capture the world-space normals of rendered object.

methods.name: calibur.shading.SHEnvironment.__init__
documentation:
The environment lighting method by Ramamoorthi et al.
"An Efficient Representation for Irradiance Environment Maps" (2001).
The environment light only depends on surface normal,
and is represented by Spherical Harmonics (SH) coefficients.

```
param sh

(9, 3) SH coefficients of the environment map.

param world_convention

target world convention for shading.
```

Two built-in environments can be found at {py:class}`SampleEnvironments`.

methods.name: calibur.shading.SHEnvironment.from_image
documentation:
Box-filtered integration of equirect environment map into SH coefficients.

```
param img

(H, W, C), preferably W = 2H.

param world_convention

target world convention for shading.
```

Experimental.

classes.name: calibur.shading.SampleEnvironments
documentation:
Example environments in the paper
"An Efficient Representation for Irradiance Environment Maps" (2001).

functions.name: calibur.graphic_utils.homogeneous
documentation: Concatenate a `1` dimension to the coords to transform it into homogeneous coordinates.

functions.name: calibur.graphic_utils.transform_point
documentation:
Transforms points with transformation matrices.
The function is not limited to 3D coords.

The output is normalized to `w=1` in homogeneous coordinates.

```
param xyz

(..., D)

param matrix

(..., D + 1, D + 1)

returns

(..., D)
```

functions.name: calibur.graphic_utils.transform_vector
documentation:
Transforms directions with transformation matrices.
The function is not limited to 3D coords.

```
param xyz

(..., D)

param matrix

(..., D + 1, D + 1)

returns

(..., D)
```

functions.name: calibur.graphic_utils.sample2d
documentation:
Bilinear sampling with UV coordinate in Blender convention.

The origin `(0, 0)` is the bottom-left corner of the image as in most UV conventions.

```
param im

(H, W, ?) image.

param xy

(..., 2), should lie in [0, 1] mostly (out-of-bounds values are clamped).

returns

(..., ?) sampled points.
```

functions.name: calibur.graphic_utils.sign2d
documentation: :meta private:

functions.name: calibur.graphic_utils.point_in_tri2d
documentation:
Decide whether points lie in 2D triangles. Arrays broadcast.

```
param pt

(..., 2) points to decide.

param v1

(..., 2) first vertex in triangles.

param v2

(..., 2) second vertex in triangles.

param v3

(..., 2) third vertex in triangles.

returns

(..., 1) boolean result of points lie in triangles.
```

functions.name: calibur.graphic_utils.magnitude
documentation:
Compute the magnitudes or lengths of vecs.

```
param vecs

(..., D).

returns

(..., 1).
```

functions.name: calibur.graphic_utils.normalized
documentation:
Safely normalize a batch of vecs.

```
param vecs

(..., D).

returns

(..., D), the same shape as input.
```

functions.name: calibur.graphic_utils.compute_tri3d_normals
documentation:
Compute face normals of triangles.

> Assumes the CCW order as forward face.

```
param tris

(..., 3, 3) where the last dimension is xyz and the second last is 3 vertices.

returns

(..., 3) of normals of each triangle.
```

functions.name: calibur.generic_utils.supercat
documentation:
Similar to `numpy.concatenate`, but supports broadcasting. For example:

``[M, 32], [N, 1, 64] -- supercat 2 --> [N, M, 96]``

functions.name: calibur.generic_utils.type_match
documentation: :meta private:

functions.name: calibur.generic_utils.container_catamorphism
documentation:
Transforms leaf elements in `list`, `dict`, `tuple`, `set` with `func`, aka. *tree-map*.
Nested containers are also supported.

functions.name: calibur.generic_utils.get_relative_path
documentation: :meta private:

functions.name: calibur.generic_utils.unbind
documentation: Unbinds an NDArray into a list of arrays along an axis.

methods.name: calibur.generic_utils.NumPyWarning.__init__
documentation:
Context manager for `numpy` warnings.
Possible options are:

- `'ignore'`
- `'warn'`
- `'raise'`
- `'call'`
- `'print'`
- `'log'`

functions.name: calibur.projection.fov_y_to_x
documentation:
Compute fov x from y.

```
param aspect

W / H.

param fovy

Vertical FOV angle in radians.

returns

Horizontal FOV angle in radians.
```

functions.name: calibur.projection.fov_to_focal
documentation:
Compute fov y and size h to focal y,

or

Compute fov x and size w to focal x.

```
param fov

Horizontal or vertical FOV angle in radians.

param size

Width or height.

returns

Focal length in the same unit as size.
```

functions.name: calibur.projection.focal_to_fov
documentation:
Inverse of `fov_to_focal`.
Angles are in radians.

functions.name: calibur.projection.projection_gl_persp
documentation:
Gets the GL projection P matrix from camera intrinsics.
The function is unit-insensitive -- the input *c/f/w/h* values can be in any unit
as long as they are the same (pixel/mm/inch/etc).

`camera_view_space_pts @ P.T -> gl_clip_space_pts`.

Applying `.xyz` / `.w` gives the GL NDC coordinates:

> `.x`
>
> : left to right => -1 to 1
>
> `.y`
>
> : bottom to up => -1 to 1
>
> `.z`
>
> : near to far => -1 to 1

```
param w

Width.

param h

Height.

param cx

Principal point x (horizontal).

param cy

Principal point y (vertical).

param fx

Focal length x (horizontal).

param fy

Focal length y (vertical).

param near

Camera near clipping plane distance.

param far

Camera far clipping plane distance.

param s

Camera shear.

returns

(4, 4) GL projection matrix
```

functions.name: calibur.projection.linear_depth_gl
documentation: GL NDC Z to linear depth formula.

functions.name: calibur.projection.intrinsic_cv
documentation:
OpenCV intrinsic matrix construction.
Input units are usually pixels for CV.

```
param cx

Principal point x (horizontal).

param cy

Principal point y (vertical).

param fx

Focal length x (horizontal).

param fy

Focal length y (vertical).

param s

Camera shear.

returns

(4, 4) GL projection matrix
```

functions.name: calibur.ndarray_extension.caster
documentation:
Cast a `numpy.ndarray` into {py:class}`GraphicsNDArray`,
but keeps other types unchanged.

functions.name: calibur.ndarray_extension.backcaster
documentation:
Cast a {py:class}`GraphicsNDArray` into `numpy.ndarray`,
but keeps other types unchanged.

functions.name: calibur.ndarray_extension.treemap_backcaster
documentation:
Cast any {py:class}`GraphicsNDArray` in (nested) lists/dicts/tuples/sets
into `numpy.ndarray`, and keeps other types unchanged.

functions.name: calibur.ndarray_extension.cast_graphics
documentation: Decorator that casts all arguments into {py:class}`GraphicsNDArray`.

functions.name: calibur.ndarray_extension.treemap_cast_graphics
documentation:
Decorator that casts all arguments of type `numpy.ndarray` into {py:class}`GraphicsNDArray`.
Also casts arrays in nested lists, dicts or tuples.

classes.name: calibur.ndarray_extension.GraphicsNDArray
documentation:
A subclass of `numpy.ndarray`.

It adds GLSL-like access to components,
e.g. `a.xyz / a.w` is a shorthand for `a[..., [0, 1, 2]] / a[..., [3]]`.

Note that single-component results retains a singleton `1` dimension.
